stem: An angle \( \theta \) in Quadrant I satisfies \( \sin\theta=\dfrac{[[n]]}{[[p]]} \) and \( \cos\theta=\dfrac{[[m]]}{[[p]]} \). Use the double-angle identity to find \( \sin(2\theta) \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the required factor of \( 2\) from the double-angle identity.
This adds \( \sin\theta\) and \( \cos\theta\) instead of multiplying them.
This uses the cosine double-angle identity \( \cos^{2}\theta-\sin^{2}\theta\) instead of the sine one.
This uses a factor of \( 4\) instead of \( 2\).
This is off by a small additive amount from the correct value.
This is off by a small additive amount (other direction) from the correct value.

stem: An angle \( \theta \) satisfies \( \tan\theta=\dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} \). Use the identity \( \cos(2\theta)=\dfrac{1-\tan^{2}\theta}{1+\tan^{2}\theta} \) to find \( \cos(2\theta) \).
distractor_rationales: This flips the sign of the numerator, negating the whole result.
This substitutes the raw ratio \( a/b\) for \( \tan^2\theta\) instead of squaring it first.
This inverts the correct fraction.
This uses the sine double-angle identity \( 2\sin\theta\cos\theta\)'s form instead of the cosine one.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct value.

stem: Use the angle-subtraction identity \( \sin(A-B)=\sin A\cos B-\cos A\sin B \) to evaluate \( \sin\!\left(\dfrac{[[k1]]\pi}{[[n1]]}\right)\cos\!\left(\dfrac{[[k2]]\pi}{[[n2]]}\right)-\cos\!\left(\dfrac{[[k1]]\pi}{[[n1]]}\right)\sin\!\left(\dfrac{[[k2]]\pi}{[[n2]]}\right) \).
solution: By the angle-subtraction identity, this expression equals \( \sin\!\left(\dfrac{[[k1]]\pi}{[[n1]]}-\dfrac{[[k2]]\pi}{[[n2]]}\right)=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the sum identity \( \sin(A+B)\) instead of the difference identity \( \sin(A-B)\).
This computes \( \cos(A-B)\) instead of \( \sin(A-B)\).
This reverses the order, computing \( \sin(B-A)=-\sin(A-B)\).
This multiplies the two sine factors directly instead of applying the subtraction identity.
This is off by a small additive amount from the correct value.
This is off by a small additive amount (other direction) from the correct value.

stem: An angle \( \theta \) in \( (0,\pi) \) satisfies \( \cos\theta=\dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} \). Use the half-angle identity to find \( \sin\dfrac{\theta}{2} \).
solution: Since \( \theta\in(0,\pi) \), \( \dfrac{\theta}{2}\in\left(0,\tfrac{\pi}{2}\right) \), so \( \sin\dfrac{\theta}{2}\ge 0 \). The half-angle identity gives \( \sin\dfrac{\theta}{2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{1-\cos\theta}{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{1-[[a]]/[[b]]}{2}}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the half-angle formula for cosine, \( \sqrt{(1+\cos\theta)/2}\), instead of sine.
This has the wrong sign; \( \sin(\theta/2)\ge 0\) since \( \theta/2\in(0,\pi/2)\).
This omits the square root, reporting \( \sin^{2}(\theta/2)\) instead of \( \sin(\theta/2)\).
This multiplies by \( 2\) instead of dividing by \( 2\) before taking the square root.
This is off by a small additive amount from the correct value.
This is off by a small additive amount (other direction) from the correct value.
